units.title: Container Element (div)
units.summary: The HTML div element is used to define a container that groups other HTML elements together.
units.source_span:
The HTML `div` element defines a container. ```html

Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

```

explanation.text: The HTML `div` element is a container—a box that holds and groups other HTML elements together. You use it to organize related content on a web page, like grouping a heading, text, and a button into one section.

analogy.text: A div is like a cardboard box that holds different items together. The box itself (the div tag) is the container, and the items inside—a label, description, and button—are like the grouped HTML elements. Just as the box keeps those items as one unit, the div keeps its child elements organized as a single container.
analogy.grounding_check: Box = 'container' (source: 'defines a container'); items inside (label, description, button) = 'other HTML elements' (source: 'groups other HTML elements'); h1, p, button in example map to the grouped content.

scenarios.text: You are building a tourism website and want to group a heading, description, and button together so they form one logical section. You wrap all three inside a `div` to keep them organized.

code_playground.html:
Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the HTML div element?
options: To define a container that groups other HTML elements together
To create a line break between elements
To add styling directly to text
To display images on a webpage
questions.explanation: The source span states that the div element 'defines a container' that groups other HTML elements together. The other options describe different HTML elements or functions not related to div's purpose.

questions.question: In the example provided, which elements are grouped inside the div container?
options: An h1 heading, a paragraph, and a button
A div, a heading, and an image
questions.explanation: The source span shows a div containing three elements: an h1 heading ('Tourism'), a p paragraph ('Plan your trip...'), and a button ('Get Started'). The other options either list only some of these elements or include elements not in the example.

questions.question: What does a div element do with the elements inside it?
options: Groups them together as a container
Converts them to a different type
questions.explanation: The source span explicitly states that div 'defines a container' that groups elements together. The other options describe actions that div does not perform according to the source material.

questions.question: Based on the example, can a div contain multiple different types of HTML elements?
options: Yes, it contains a heading, paragraph, and button together
No, a div can only hold one type of element
No, a div can only hold text elements
Yes, but only if they are the same type
questions.explanation: The source example shows a div containing an h1, a p, and a button—three different element types grouped together. This demonstrates that div can group multiple different types of elements, not just one type.

units.title: CSS Rule Syntax
units.summary: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

explanation.text: A CSS rule is a block of code that styles elements on a web page. It has two main parts: a selector (which picks which elements to style) and a declaration block (the curly braces containing one or more property-value pairs, where each property is followed by a colon, then a value, then a semicolon).

analogy.text: A CSS rule is like a mailing label on a package. The address on the label is like the selector—it identifies which package (element) you're targeting. The contents listed inside the package are like the property-value pairs inside the curly braces—each item in the package (each property: value) describes something specific about what's inside.
analogy.grounding_check: selector → 'selector' in source; property-value pairs → 'property1: value1; property2: value2' in source; curly braces block → 'block containing one or more property-value pairs' in source

scenarios.text: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

questions.question: What two main parts make up a CSS rule?
options: A selector and a declaration block
questions.explanation: The source shows a CSS rule has a selector (like a class or element name) followed by curly braces containing declarations. A property-value pair is just one piece inside the block, not the two main parts of the rule itself.

questions.question: In a CSS rule, what goes inside the curly braces?
questions.explanation: The source span shows the curly braces contain property-value pairs separated by semicolons. The selector comes before the braces, not inside them.

questions.question: What character ends each property-value pair in a CSS rule?
questions.explanation: The source shows each property-value pair ends with a semicolon (for example, `property1: value1;`). A colon separates the property from the value, not the pair from the next one.

questions.question: How many property-value pairs must a CSS rule contain?
questions.explanation: The source states the declaration block contains 'one or more property-value pairs,' meaning a rule can have just one pair or multiple pairs. The phrase 'one or more' explicitly rules out zero and doesn't require exactly two.

units.title: Text Align Property
units.summary: The CSS text-align property controls the horizontal alignment of text within an HTML element using values like center, left, or right.
units.source_span:
The CSS `text-align` property specifies the horizontal alignment of the text in an HTML element.

```css
.h-center {
  text-align: center;
}
```

| Value  | Description                   |
| ------ | ----------------------------- |
| center | Aligns the text to the center |
| left   | Aligns the text to the left   |
| right  | Aligns the text to the right  |

explanation.text: The CSS `text-align` property controls where text lines up horizontally inside an HTML element. You choose a value like `center`, `left`, or `right` to position the text. This property acts on the main axis (horizontal direction) within the element.

analogy.text: Imagine you have a poster board and want to position a line of text on it. The poster board is like the HTML element, and the text-align property is like your decision about where to place the text. If you choose center, the text sits in the middle of the board; if you choose left, it sits at the left edge; if you choose right, it sits at the right edge—just like the property's values (center, left, right) control where the text aligns horizontally within the element.
analogy.grounding_check: poster board = HTML element (source: 'in an HTML element'); text positioning decision = text-align property (source: 'specifies the horizontal alignment'); center/left/right placement = the three values and their effects (source: table showing center/left/right descriptions)

scenarios.text: You want to center a heading on a webpage so it stands out. You add `text-align: center;` to the heading's CSS class, and the text moves to the middle of its container.

code_playground.html:
Welcome to My Site

This is regular paragraph text below the heading.

questions.question: What does the CSS text-align property control?
questions.explanation: The source states that text-align specifies the horizontal alignment of text. Vertical alignment, text size, and color are controlled by different CSS properties, not text-align.

questions.question: Which value would you use with text-align to move text to the middle of an element?
questions.explanation: The source table shows that 'center' aligns text to the center. 'Middle' is not a valid text-align value; the other options are not real CSS values.

questions.question: What does text-align: left do?
options: Aligns the text to the left
Aligns the text to the right
questions.explanation: The source table explicitly states that the 'left' value aligns text to the left. The other options describe different text-align values or properties that don't exist.

questions.question: In the example `.h-center { text-align: center; }`, which axis does text-align control?
questions.explanation: The source states that text-align specifies horizontal alignment. Vertical, diagonal, and depth are different axes that text-align does not control.
